title: Chapter 7: Luxury Freedom
content: <!-- Speed: Sprint | D1+D8 -->
She took the coat from the rack and the alarm didn't sound.

Cashmere. Italian. Three thousand dollars, according to the tag — which she'd studied with the reverence of someone who'd never spent more than forty dollars on a single piece of clothing. The fabric was cloud-colored, the weight of it surprising, like wearing a small animal that had decided to trust her.
She draped it over her arm and walked out of Bendel's through the front door.

A sales associate was helping a customer two feet away. Another was folding scarves by the entrance. The security sensor at the door — the white plastic gate that was supposed to scream when merchandise passed through — stayed silent.
Wren stepped onto Fifth Avenue in a stolen cashmere coat and the October sun hit her like a reward.
The first three days after the Harrington Grand, she'd been cautious. Testing. Pushing at the edges of her new condition like a kid pressing on a bruise — does it still hurt? Is it still real?
By day four, she'd stopped testing.
The rules were simple: she could take anything. She could go anywhere. She could stand in the middle of any room and no one would ask her name.
So she did.

The bakery on Spring Street — the one with the line that wrapped around the block, the one she'd walked past a hundred times because the wait was forty minutes and forty minutes standing still among strangers was a type of torture she couldn't afford — she walked right in. Reached past the rope. Stood at the counter while a woman in a silk scarf ordered a latte with oat milk and extra foam.

Wren took a croissant from the display. Still warm. The butter layers flaked apart in her fingers. She ate it standing up, crumbs falling onto the marble floor, while the barista turned in a slow circle looking for the source of the mess.
She almost felt guilty.
Almost.
The restaurant was called Veris. Tasting menu only. Twelve courses. Two hundred and eighty dollars per person, wine pairing extra.

She'd read about it on a food blog she followed — the kind where every dish was described like a small miracle, where the photography made you hungry and resentful in equal measure. She'd bookmarked it once, years ago, in a tab she never closed. A fantasy meal for a fantasy version of herself.
Now she sat at an empty two-top near the kitchen. The table had been set — linen napkin, three forks, a water glass filled halfway. A couple had apparently canceled.

The first course arrived. Hamachi crudo with yuzu and shiso. The waiter set it down at the empty place setting, glanced around for the guest who should have been sitting there, frowned, and retreated.
Wren picked up the fork.
The fish was clean and bright, the yuzu a tiny explosion of citrus that made her palate sit up and pay attention. She ate it in four bites. Set the fork down. Waited.

The second course came. A couple at the next table leaned toward each other, whispering about the lobster bisque. Wren ate a burrata with heirloom tomatoes that tasted like they'd been grown in someone's backyard, not shipped in a truck.
Third course. Fourth. By the sixth — a Wagyu slider on a brioche bun so buttery it dissolved — she stopped counting. She just ate.
A tablespoon of caviar. A sphere of mozzarella suspended in basil oil. A chocolate fondant that broke open to reveal a molten center the color of dark soil.
She ate like someone who'd been starving. Which she had. Not for food — for this. For the experience of being somewhere she was never supposed to be.

When the waiter came to clear the twelfth plate, Wren was gone. She'd slipped out through the service corridor, past the kitchen, where a dishwasher looked up at the flicker of movement and then back down at his sink.
The coat. The meal. These were appetizers.

She walked into a luxury condo open house on the Upper West Side — a penthouse with floor-to-ceiling windows and a kitchen island the size of her bathroom. The broker was showing a young couple, talking about natural light and pre-war bones and proximity to the park.

Wren walked through every room. Opened closets. Flushed the toilet in the master bathroom just to hear the sound it made — expensive, quiet, like a whisper of agreement. She sat on the terrace and looked at the park, at the trees turning copper and gold, and felt the cold air on her face and the silence of not being asked to justify her presence.
She could live here. She could live anywhere.
But the taking changed.
It stopped being enough to take things. She needed to leave something.

It started small. A drawing on the back of a receipt, left on the counter at Veris. A sketch of the chandelier from the Harrington Grand, tucked into the pocket of the cashmere coat she'd stolen — which she then hung back on the rack at Bendel's, slightly warmer than before, carrying a secret in its lining.

Then bigger. She broke into a gallery after hours — the lock was laughable — and left a drawing on a blank wall. A small one, in pencil, in the corner, where no one would notice it for weeks. A woman dissolving at the edges, her outline flickering like a bad signal.
She left another in a library book. A third in the condensation on a steamed-up window at a coffee shop — drawing with her fingertip, watching it vanish as the glass dried.
No one would see them. That was the point. That was the counterpoint. The world couldn't see her, so she signed it instead. *I was here. I was here. I was here.*
The city was a sketchbook and she was marking every page.

At a boutique on Madison — the kind with one garment per rack and a saleswoman who looked like she'd been carved from marble — she tried on a wool overcoat, charcoal gray, oversized. It fit the way her hoodie never quite did: like armor that had been made for her.
She stood in front of the three-way mirror and watched her reflection.
It flickered.
There — then gone — then there again, like a candle in a draft. She could see herself, but only barely. The mirror showed a smear, a suggestion, an outline that couldn't commit to being a person.
She pulled the coat tighter.
In the mirror's gap — the space where her reflection should have been solid — she saw the saleswoman behind her, texting, perfectly visible through the space Wren's body should have filled.
Wren stepped back from the mirror.
The coat was beautiful. She kept it on and walked out.
Thirteen days. That's how long it had been since the first pill.

She sat on a bench in Central Park, eating a bagel she hadn't paid for, wearing a coat that didn't belong to her, and she felt — what? Free? Yes. Powerful? That too. But underneath it, a thin current of something she didn't want to name.
Hunger.

Not for food. She was full. Not for things — she'd taken enough to outfit a small apartment. The hunger was for something the things couldn't provide. A witness. Someone to see her standing in the middle of the Harrington Grand, to notice the drawing she left in the library book, to say: *You were here. I saw you.*
She pulled the pill bottle from her pocket. Shook it. Sixteen left.

She'd been taking two a day. Sometimes two and a half, cracking a capsule and dumping half the powder under her tongue where it absorbed fast and bitter. The math was simple. Sixteen pills. Eight days, maybe less.
The bottle was lighter every time she held it. She could feel the space where the pills had been — empty plastic rattling, a hollow sound.
She put the bottle away.

The bagel was gone. She'd eaten it without tasting the second half. The park was full of people — joggers, dog walkers, a busker playing violin near Bethesda Fountain, the music carrying across the water, thin and sweet and achingly lonely.
She sat there until the sun went down, and the hunger stayed.
